mechanism_narrative: Rising total nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) reflects a strengthening labor market which drives consumer spending and corporate earnings, leading to higher S&P 500 prices over a 20-day horizon. This is a persistent trend signal active at all times.
evidence_refs: fact:labor market expansion correlates with equity returns in historical data up to 2007
failure_condition: Walk-forward Spearman rank correlation between monthly change in PAYEMS and subsequent 20-day S&P 500 return is negative or below 0.10 over any 2-year window.

mechanism_narrative: When the civilian unemployment rate (UNRATE) rises above 4.5%, it signals labor market deterioration that leads to reduced consumer spending and earnings estimates, pushing S&P 500 prices lower over the subsequent 20 trading days.
evidence_refs: fact:historical reversals from low unemployment to rising unemployment preceded equity declines in 2000 and 2007
failure_condition: In at least 3 out of 5 instances where UNRATE rises above 4.5%, the S&P 500 produces positive 20-day returns, or the average return difference between triggered and non-triggered periods is not statistically significant at 95% confidence.

mechanism_narrative: Declining housing starts (HOUST) concurrent with still-rising payrolls (PAYEMS) creates a divergence indicating an impending economic slowdown. The housing weakness erodes construction employment and consumer wealth, while payrolls lag, but the combined signal warns of a contraction that will reduce S&P 500 earnings and valuations over a 60-day horizon.
candidate_trigger: HOUST month-over-month decline for 3 consecutive months while PAYEMS remains positive month-over-month
evidence_refs: fact:housing starts peaked in early 2006 and declined through 2007 while employment was still expanding
failure_condition: In cases where HOUST declines for 3+ months while PAYEMS still rises, the S&P 500 returns positive in more than 60% of 60-day forward windows, or the average return is not significantly below the unconditional mean.

mechanism_narrative: In an environment where consumer price inflation (CPIAUCSL) is moderate (year-over-year change below 3%), rising retail sales (RSAFS) indicate strong real consumption growth that boosts corporate revenues and S&P 500 earnings, leading to positive forward returns over 20 days. The regime condition filters out periods where inflation distorts signals.
candidate_trigger: CPIAUCSL YoY change < 3% and RSAFS month-over-month growth > 0.5%
evidence_refs: fact:low inflation environment combined with retail strength was a positive equity environment in mid-2000s
failure_condition: When both conditions are met, the 20-day S&P 500 return is negative in more than 50% of instances, or the average return is not significantly above zero (t-test p > 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: Industrial production (INDPRO) measures real output of manufacturing, mining, and utilities. A rising index signals expanding supply capacity and economic strength, which directly supports corporate earnings and equity valuations over a short 5-day horizon. The signal is always active.
evidence_refs: fact:industrial production leads GDP growth and earnings with short lags
failure_condition: The Rank IC of INDPRO month-over-month change on subsequent 5-day SPX returns is below 0.05 over any 1-year rolling window.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) falling below 1,500 thousand units indicates a severe contraction in residential investment that has broad negative spillovers to consumer spending, banking, and employment, leading to a sustained decline in S&P 500 over a 60-day horizon.
evidence_refs: fact:housing starts peaked at 2273 in Jan 2006 and declined; as of May 2007 it was 1679
failure_condition: When HOUST is below 1500, the average 60-day S&P 500 return is positive or not significantly different from the unconditional mean at the 90% confidence level.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) is below the year-over-year change in consumer price index (CPIAUCSL), real interest rates are negative. Negative real rates historically signal policy accommodation that is insufficient to counteract inflation fears, ultimately eroding equity valuations as future cash flows are discounted at higher nominal rates. This interaction predicts negative S&P 500 returns over 20 days.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS < CPIAUCSL YoY change (in percentage points)
evidence_refs: fact:negative real rates in early 2000s preceded the 2000 crash; in 2007 real rates turned negative again with rising inflation
failure_condition: When real rates are negative, the 20-day S&P 500 return is positive in more than 60% of instances, or the average return is not significantly less than periods with positive real rates (p < 0.10).

mechanism_narrative: In a tight labor market regime where the unemployment rate (UNRATE) is below 4%, continued strong payroll growth (PAYEMS) signals an overheating economy that will prompt monetary tightening and eventual slowdown, leading to lower S&P 500 prices over a 20-day horizon. The regime condition isolates the period when further labor strength is counterproductive.
evidence_refs: fact:when unemployment below 4%, further employment gains often precede market peaks as seen in 2000 and 2007
failure_condition: During periods of UNRATE < 4%, the Rank IC between month-over-month PAYEMS growth and subsequent 20-day SPX returns is positive or above 0.05, or the average return following above-median PAYEMS growth is not significantly negative.
